name: Inner Authority
tagline: Speak and act from your true self instead of your fear of judgment.
situations: Freezing up when all eyes are on you
Afraid of being judged before you speak
Social anxiety before a presentation or group
summary: Inner Authority is Stutz's tool for insecurity and the fear of judgment. You picture your most insecure, hidden self — the 'Shadow' — standing beside you, and you speak in unison with it, drawing confidence from full self-acceptance rather than the crowd's approval. It shifts the source of your authority from outside to inside.
howItWorks: Social anxiety comes from outsourcing your worth to other people's judgment, which puts your sense of self at their mercy. Inner Authority reclaims it by uniting you with the very part you're ashamed of, your Shadow. When you accept and speak alongside that hidden self, approval-seeking loses its grip because you're no longer dependent on the audience. Confidence becomes a function of self-acceptance, not performance.

steps.instruction: Picture your Shadow — the insecure, awkward, hidden part of you — standing just beside you.
Turn toward it and accept it completely, as it is.
Face the situation together, and speak or act in unison with your Shadow.
Draw your authority from this union, not from the crowd's reaction.

whenToUse: Before speaking in front of others
When fear of judgment makes you freeze or shrink
Social anxiety before a meeting, date, or presentation
whenNotToUse: As a substitute for preparation when a task needs real skill
If self-focus increases anxiety, ground externally first
It complements, not replaces, treatment for severe social anxiety
tips: The acceptance of the Shadow is what generates the confidence
Practice it on small social moments before big ones
Pair with a slow breath to settle the body first
Confidence here is internal — it doesn't require feeling fearless

evidence.summary: A clinical-practice tool consistent with self-acceptance and exposure principles; reducing reliance on external approval is supported in social-anxiety treatment research.
citations: Stutz, P. & Michels, B. (2012). The Tools. Spiegel & Grau.
